content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I'll send you a message when I ___ (get) home tonight.
distractor_rationale: will is not used in a future time clause introduced by 'when' — this is the most common learner error
past simple 'got' is impossible in a future time clause referring to a future event tonight
present continuous implies an already-arranged ongoing action, not a future point-in-time arrival

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Please wait here ___ the doctor is ready to see you.
distractor_rationale: 'when' marks the point at which something happens, not a continuous period of waiting up to that point
'while' introduces a simultaneous action, but waiting and the doctor being ready are not simultaneous events
'after' means following a completed event, which is the opposite of what the sentence requires

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom always checks his phone ___ he eats his breakfast in the morning.
distractor_rationale: 'until' marks the end point of an action, but checking a phone and eating breakfast happen at the same time
'before' means prior to eating, but the sentence describes two simultaneous habitual actions
'as soon as' means immediately after, but the two actions overlap rather than one triggering the other

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: She will not leave the office ___ she finishes writing the report for her manager.
distractor_rationale: 'when' marks the moment something happens, not a continuous state that continues up to a future point
'as soon as' means she would leave immediately upon finishing, which contradicts the negative 'will not leave'
'after' would mean she stays following the report, which reverses the intended meaning

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: As soon as the train ___ (arrive) at the station, we'll pick up our bags and head to the hotel.
distractor_rationale: will is not used in a future time clause introduced by 'as soon as' — this is a direct learner error
past simple 'arrived' refers to a completed past event, but the sentence is about a future journey
present perfect 'has arrived' could theoretically signal completion, but 'as soon as' requires present simple in future time clauses, not present perfect

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Make sure you turn off all the lights ___ you leave the house for your holiday.
distractor_rationale: 'while' requires two simultaneous actions, but turning off lights and leaving happen in sequence, not at the same time
'until' would mean the lights stay on up to the moment of leaving, which contradicts the instruction to turn them off
'when' would mean turning them off at the exact moment of leaving, but the instruction implies doing it as a prior step

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: ___ the children were playing in the garden, their parents were preparing lunch inside the house.
distractor_rationale: 'when' introduces a point-in-time event, but both actions here are ongoing and simultaneous, which requires 'while'
'after' means one action follows the completion of another, but preparing lunch and playing happen at the same time
'until' marks the end point of a duration, not two parallel ongoing actions

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: By the time the rescue team ___ (reach) the village, the flooding had already damaged most of the houses.
distractor_rationale: past perfect is reserved for the earlier action in a past sequence; reaching the village is the later action, so simple past is required
past continuous 'was reaching' describes an ongoing action, but the sentence requires a completed arrival at a specific point
present perfect cannot be used for a completed past sequence where both events are clearly in the past

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna will have finished packing her suitcase ___ her taxi arrives to take her to the airport.
distractor_rationale: 'as soon as' means immediately when the taxi arrives, but the future perfect 'will have finished' signals completion before that moment, which only 'by the time' expresses
'while' requires two simultaneous ongoing actions, but packing will be completed before the taxi arrives, not during
'until' would mean she continues packing up to the taxi's arrival, contradicting the future perfect which shows prior completion

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The project manager wants everyone to review the draft carefully ___ submitting the final version to the client next Friday.
distractor_rationale: 'after' reverses the required sequence — reviewing must happen prior to submission, not following it
'until' would mean the team reviews continuously up to the moment of submission without a clear stopping point, which does not match the instruction to review carefully as a prior step
'while' implies reviewing and submitting happen simultaneously, but you cannot submit and review at the same time

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I'll turn off the lights before I ___ (leave) the house tonight.
distractor_rationale: will is not used in a subordinate time clause introduced by before — this is the most common learner error on this structure
past simple cannot be used in a future time clause; the action has not happened yet
present continuous in a time clause implies an ongoing background action, but leaving is a single completed point, making this contextually wrong

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children were playing in the garden ___ it started to rain heavily.
distractor_rationale: while introduces a simultaneous ongoing action, but the rain starting is a sudden point-in-time event that interrupted the playing, not a parallel activity

until means 'up to the point that', which would imply the children stopped playing only at the moment of rain and no earlier — the sentence describes an interruption, not a limit, making until wrong here
before would mean the playing happened earlier than the rain, contradicting the sense that the rain interrupted the playing while it was in progress

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Please wait here ___ the doctor calls your name — she won't be long.
distractor_rationale: when refers to a specific point in time but does not carry the meaning of continuous waiting up to that point, so 'wait here when the doctor calls' is logically incoherent as a command
while describes two simultaneous ongoing actions, but waiting and the doctor calling are not simultaneous — calling is the endpoint of the waiting
after means the waiting would happen following the call, which reverses the intended sequence entirely

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: As soon as Mark ___ (finish) his report, he will send it to the whole team.
distractor_rationale: will is not permitted in a subordinate time clause introduced by as soon as — learners frequently transfer the future meaning of the main clause into the time clause
past simple signals a completed past event, but the context is clearly future — Mark has not finished yet
present perfect continuous describes an ongoing activity with duration, but as soon as requires a simple completion point, not a duration, making this form semantically incompatible

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: By the time Anna ___ (get) back from her business trip, her colleagues will have already presented the results.
distractor_rationale: will is not used in a time clause introduced by by the time when referring to a future event — the time clause takes present simple even though the main clause uses future perfect
past simple is only correct when the main clause describes a past completed event; here the main clause uses future perfect, confirming the whole sentence is future-oriented
present perfect suggests the action is already complete relative to now, but the context makes clear Anna has not yet returned — the sentence projects a future scenario

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I'll wait here until the bus ___.
distractor_rationale: using 'will' in a time clause after 'until' is a core learner error — present simple is required
past tense cannot be used in a future time clause introduced by 'until'
present continuous suggests a temporary action in progress, not the single arrival point that 'until' requires

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She'll turn off the lights before she ___ the house.
distractor_rationale: using 'will' in the time clause after 'before' is a common learner error — present simple is required
past tense is incorrect in a future time clause; the action has not happened yet
a bare '-ing' form cannot function as the main verb in a subordinate clause without an auxiliary

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom was reading a book ___ his sister watched television in the next room.
distractor_rationale: 'when' signals a point in time or a single event, not two simultaneous ongoing actions
'until' marks the endpoint of an action, not two actions happening at the same time
'after' signals a sequence where one action follows another, not two simultaneous actions

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We'll start cooking dinner as soon as David ___ from the supermarket.
distractor_rationale: using 'will' in a time clause after 'as soon as' is a typical learner error — present simple is required
past tense is incorrect because the action is still in the future at the time of speaking
present continuous implies an action in progress right now, not a future arrival point triggered by 'as soon as'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Please don't say anything to Maria ___ I have spoken to her manager first.
distractor_rationale: 'when' marks the moment something happens, not the endpoint up to which an action is withheld
'while' describes two simultaneous actions, not a restriction lasting up to a future point
'after' would mean the talking should not happen following the conversation, which contradicts the intended meaning of waiting

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: After she ___ her homework, Anna went outside to play with her friends.
distractor_rationale: present simple is incorrect here because the main clause is in the past; the time clause must also be past
using 'will' in a time clause is always incorrect, and the main clause is already in the past tense
past continuous suggests an ongoing action, but 'after' requires a completed action before the next event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The children were playing loudly ___ the teacher suddenly walked into the classroom.
distractor_rationale: 'while' requires two simultaneous ongoing actions, but the teacher's entry is a single interrupting event, not an ongoing action
'until' marks an endpoint, not an interrupting moment during an ongoing activity
'before' would mean the playing happened prior to the entry, removing the sense of interruption that the sentence conveys

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: By the time the repair team ___ the office tomorrow, the manager will have already left for the airport.
distractor_rationale: using 'will' in a time clause introduced by 'by the time' is a persistent learner error — present simple is required in future time clauses
past simple is incorrect because the scenario is set in the future; 'tomorrow' confirms this
present perfect is not used in future time clauses introduced by 'by the time' when the reference is a future point; present simple is required

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: You should save your work regularly ___ the computer crashes and you lose everything you have typed.
distractor_rationale: 'until' marks a continuous state up to a point, not a precautionary action taken to prevent a future event
'while' describes simultaneous actions, but saving work is done to prevent the crash, not at the same time as it
'as soon as' means immediately after the crash, but saving after a crash is too late — the sentence requires a precautionary 'before'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The project manager will not approve the final report until the team ___ all the necessary changes to the document.
distractor_rationale: using 'will have made' in the time clause after 'until' is incorrect — future perfect is not used in time clauses; present perfect is required to show completion before the main event
past simple cannot be used in a future time clause; the action of making changes has not happened yet
present simple 'makes' does not convey the sense of completed action that the context requires; present perfect 'has made' signals all changes must be fully done before approval

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I will call you when I will get home from work.
content.error_sentence: I will call you when I will get home from work.
content.corrected_sentence: I will call you when I get home from work.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Please wait here until the doctor will call your name.
content.correct_answer: until the doctor calls your name
content.error_sentence: Please wait here until the doctor will call your name.
content.corrected_sentence: Please wait here until the doctor calls your name.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She always reads her messages while she will eat breakfast in the morning.
content.error_sentence: She always reads her messages while she will eat breakfast in the morning.
content.corrected_sentence: She always reads her messages while she eats breakfast in the morning.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Make sure you turn off all the lights before you will leave the office tonight.
content.error_sentence: Make sure you turn off all the lights before you will leave the office tonight.
content.corrected_sentence: Make sure you turn off all the lights before you leave the office tonight.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom listened to music when he was cooking dinner last night, so he didn't hear the phone.
content.error_sentence: Tom listened to music when he was cooking dinner last night, so he didn't hear the phone.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom listened to music while he was cooking dinner last night, so he didn't hear the phone.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: As soon as the package will arrive, we can start assembling the furniture in the living room.
content.correct_answer: as soon as the package arrives
content.error_sentence: As soon as the package will arrive, we can start assembling the furniture in the living room.
content.corrected_sentence: As soon as the package arrives, we can start assembling the furniture in the living room.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: By the time we arrived at the cinema, the film already started and the lights were down.
content.error_sentence: By the time we arrived at the cinema, the film already started and the lights were down.
content.corrected_sentence: By the time we arrived at the cinema, the film had already started and the lights were down.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The children have to stay in the classroom until the rain will stop and the playground is dry.
content.error_sentence: The children have to stay in the classroom until the rain will stop and the playground is dry.
content.corrected_sentence: The children have to stay in the classroom until the rain stops and the playground is dry.
